internal_links_used.anchor: Florida state income tax & reemployment tax

internal_links_used.context: links-rail · sibling state · peer minimalist regime

high_concern_notes: Steen's prompt was provisional. Verified primary sources and refined: (a) public-policy exception anchor case is Hartley v. Ocean Reef Club, Inc., 476 So. 2d 1327 (Fla. 3d DCA 1985), not a Florida Supreme Court decision; (b) Florida sits with Georgia, Louisiana, Rhode Island as the four states rejecting the broad public-policy exception; (c) FCRA filing window with FCHR is 365 days (NOT the 180-day equivalent of Title VII's EEOC clock — it's longer); (d) Florida is an EEOC deferral state via the FCHR, so the EEOC clock is 300 days, not 180; (e) Florida HAS a private-sector Whistleblower Act (FS § 448.102, 10+ employees), unlike Alabama which only covers public employees; (f) HB 1407 (effective 1 July 2026) clarifies the EEOC NRTS as a statute-of-limitations trigger for FCRA civil actions.

sources.title: Hartley v. Ocean Reef Club, Inc., 476 So. 2d 1327 (Fla. 3d DCA 1985)
Fla. Stat. § 448.102 — Florida Private Sector Whistleblower Act
Fla. Stat. Chapter 760 — Florida Civil Rights Act
Amended Florida Civil Rights Act (HB 1407, effective 1 July 2026) — Jackson Lewis analysis
US DOL — Wage and Hour Division (FLSA final pay default)
US DOL — Federal WARN Act (29 USC § 2102)
EEOC — Time limits for filing a charge (300 days in deferral states)
